coredata.dc:title: A novel functionalized nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped nanocarbon dots for the fluorescence detection and photocatalysis degradation of tetracycline antibiotics

coredata.dc:description: Monitoring and eliminating pollutants are critical for environmental remediation. In this study, nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped nanocarbon dots (NS-NCDs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using ascorbic acid and thiosemicarbazide as precursors. The introduction of nitrogen and sulfur atoms through doping altered the electron configuration and nanostructure of the carbon dots, resulting in strong blue fluorescence (quantum yield: 11.32 %). These NS-NCDs functioned as dual-functional agents for both detecting and degrading tetracycline antibiotics (TCs). Under UV light, the fluorescence color of the NS-NCDs transitioned from blue to colorless with increasing TCs concentration. Fluorescence quenching of NS-NCDs by TCs was mediated synergistically by the inner filter effect (IFE) and electron transfer, with IFE contributing 76 %, 75 %, and 71 % of the total quenching efficiency for tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and chlortetracycline (CTC), respectively. This dual-mechanism enabled ultrasensitive detection of TCs, achieving limits of detection (LODs) as low as 0.21 μM (TC), 0.28 μM (OTC), and 0.16 μM (CTC), which are well below regulatory thresholds for environmental antibiotic residues. The NS-NCDs probe also exhibited high selectivity and anti-interference performance in complex matrices. Furthermore, NS-NCDs serve as a catalyst to rapidly activate peroxymonosulfate (PMS) under sunlight for the degradation of TCs, achieving degradation rates exceeding 90 % within 60 min. TCs degradation is driven by the oxidation of free radicals and electron transfer from TCs to PMS, facilitated by NS-NCDs. This study demonstrates the bifunctional role of NS-NCDs in real-time monitoring and photocatalytic degradation of TCs, providing an innovative strategy to combat antibiotic contamination.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Monitoring and eliminating pollutants are critical for environmental remediation. In this study, nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped nanocarbon dots (NS-NCDs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using ascorbic acid and thiosemicarbazide as precursors. The introduction of nitrogen and sulfur atoms through doping altered the electron configuration and nanostructure of the carbon dots, resulting in strong blue fluorescence (quantum yield: 11.32 %). These NS-NCDs functioned as dual-functional agents for both detecting and degrading tetracycline antibiotics (TCs). Under UV light, the fluorescence color of the NS-NCDs transitioned from blue to colorless with increasing TCs concentration. Fluorescence quenching of NS-NCDs by TCs was mediated synergistically by the inner filter effect (IFE) and electron transfer, with IFE contributing 76 %, 75 %, and 71 % of the total quenching efficiency for tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and chlortetracycline (CTC), respectively. This dual-mechanism enabled ultrasensitive detection of TCs, achieving limits of detection (LODs) as low as 0.21 μM (TC), 0.28 μM (OTC), and 0.16 μM (CTC), which are well below regulatory thresholds for environmental antibiotic residues. The NS-NCDs probe also exhibited high selectivity and anti-interference performance in complex matrices. Furthermore, NS-NCDs serve as a catalyst to rapidly activate peroxymonosulfate (PMS) under sunlight for the degradation of TCs, achieving degradation rates exceeding 90 % within 60 min. TCs degradation is driven by the oxidation of free radicals and electron transfer from TCs to PMS, facilitated by NS-NCDs. This study demonstrates the bifunctional role of NS-NCDs in real-time monitoring and photocatalytic degradation of TCs, providing an innovative strategy to combat antibiotic contamination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental problems caused by antibiotics, widely used in medical treatments, agriculture, and aquaculture, have gained increasing attention. Global antibiotic consumption by humans witnessed a 65 % upsurge from 2000 to 2015, and the daily consumption increased from 22.1 to 34.8 billion (Klein et al., 2018). According to the current trend, consumption is projected to increase by 200 % by 2030 (Scaria et al., 2021). Moreover, different types of antibiotics have been found in soils, rivers, lakes, and drinking water across many regions worldwide, with concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 1 mg/L (Liu et al., 2020). Antibiotics are becoming a concern for new environmental pollutants because of their potential negative impacts on the environment and physical well-being. Tetracyclines (TCs), including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and chlortetracycline (CTC), constitute a common group of broad-spectrum antibiotics (Wang et al., 2022a). TCs follow an ecotoxicological trajectory, bioaccumulating in humans through ecosystems and resulting in gastrointestinal, immune, and allergic health risks for susceptible individuals (Feng et al., 2015; Qi et al., 2019). Ecologically, aquatic TCs residues exert photosynthetic inhibition on algae and erode planktonic biodiversity, whereas soil TCs persistence perturbs microbial nutrient flux, antagonizes probiotic bacteria, and selects for antibiotic resistance determinants (Zainab et al., 2020). Hence, it is crucial to develop a multifunctional method with high performance for detecting and removing TCs.

Researchers have developed various methods for detecting TCs, such as high-performance liquid chromatography, chromatography-mass spectrometry, enzyme-linked immunoassay, and electrochemical assays (Berendsen et al., 2015; Dargahi et al., 2023; Jin et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021b). However, these techniques require precision instruments, complex sample preparation, and expert skills, limiting their practical use. Fluorescence probe-based sensing technology has attracted attention due to its high sensitivity and fast response. In particular, visual fluorescence probes have shown distinctive benefits by transforming the analyte concentration information into discernible fluorescence color changes. First, they enabled semi-quantitative analysis without requiring complex instruments, which greatly lowered detection costs; second, the color gradient changes offered clear concentration distribution data, facilitating rapid on-site decision-making by non-specialists (Wang et al., 2022b). Fluorescence probes include organic dyes, metal nanoclusters (MNCs), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), and nanocarbon dots (NCDs) (Chen et al., 2022; Li et al., 2020a; Liu et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2023). Organic dyes have poor photochemical stability, photobleaching, and environmental pollution potential (Tanwar et al., 2016). MNCs and MOFs are limited by heavy metal ions, which can cause biological toxicity. Since NCDs were first discovered in 2004 by Xu et al., they have gained attention for their wide raw material sources, simple synthesis, environmental friendliness, excellent light stability, and tunable optical properties (Reshma and Mohanan, 2019; Sun et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2004). However, their low quantum yield (QY) limits their use in TC detection. To overcome this, doping NCDs with elements like boron (B), phosphorus (P), sulfur (S), and nitrogen (N) has been shown to improve fluorescence QY (Du and Guo, 2016; Miao et al., 2020; Park et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2016). The energy gap between the highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals affects properties of NCDs, which can be adjusted by introducing dopants (Ansari et al., 2021). For example, B- and P-doped NCDs enhance luminescence by creating new defects on the surface (Bourlinos et al., 2015; Li et al., 2017b). S-doped NCDs provide an energy trap state, preventing self-quenching (Wang et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2015). Nitrogen-doped NCDs have been used to detect Hg2+ and TC in tap water with recovery rates of 98.7 %–100.17 % (R. Thara et al., 2022). Furthermore, researchers have focused on the doping of multiple heteroatoms and have found that the synergistic effects of diverse heteroatoms can generate distinctive electronic structures. The introduction of multiple heteroatoms into NCDs can lead to a more uniform bandgap, generating additional surface defects and thereby reducing non-radiative recombination while enhancing the fluorescence intensity (Cui et al., 2022; Duan et al., 2023). Although numerous studies have investigated NCDs doping, most have focused on single-element-doped NCDs, while there are few studies on multiple-element-doped NCDs.

Recently, the notable advantages of peroxymonosulfate (PMS)-based advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) in degrading and mineralizing stubborn organic compounds have garnered considerable attention (Wang et al., 2021a; Yang et al., 2019). The activation of PMS is a key part of the process, and the main approaches include thermal processing, ultrasonic radiation, catalysis, and photocatalysis (Pang et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2025; Zhang et al., 2024). Among these, PMS activation using photocatalysts under sunlight has been found to hold promising application potential owing to its high efficiency. NCDs exhibit extraordinary light absorption in the UV and visible regions, enabling the effective modulation of fluorescence emission wavelengths and the conversion of low-energy photons into high-energy photons, which makes them effective photocatalysts in widely used methods. Compared to traditional metal catalysts, NCDs offer milder reaction conditions and decrease the risk of secondary pollution, thus representing an eco-friendly alternative (Niu et al., 2024). For example, an effective near-infrared (NIR) light-driven NCDs photocatalyst was developed by Li et al., which selectively oxidized benzyl alcohol to benzaldehyde (Li et al., 2013). Lei et al. synthesized NCDs with excellent emission properties and crystallinity for the redox-neutral boronation of aryl diazonium salts in aqueous solutions under visible light (Lei et al., 2020). He et al. synthesized NCDs that functioned as polymer carbon nitride, which effectively activated PMS to degrade bisphenol An under visible-light irradiation (He et al., 2023). However, the degradation effects of NCDs on related pollutants have been primarily addressed by existing research, with little investigation into the photocatalytic properties of multiple-atom-doped NCDs. Multi-atom doping can modify both the surface properties and electronic distribution of NCDs; thus, clarifying their photocatalytic performance remains an important issue.

In the present study, the conventional hydrothermal method was employed, which offers the advantage of completing the entire reaction in one step using low-cost precursors under ordinary hydrothermal conditions, to prepare N- and S-co-doped NCDs (NS-NCDs) from ascorbic acid (ASA) and thiosemicarbazide. The results of the interaction between NS-NCDs and a variety of interfering ions indicated that they could undergo specific quenching by TC, OTC, and CTC. Owing to the internal filtering effect (IFE) and electron transfer mechanism, the NS-NCDs fluorescence sensor possesses a broad detection range and a low detection limit in the presence of TCs. Furthermore, NS-NCDs rapidly activated PMS under sunlight to degrade TCs, with removal rates of over 90 % for the three types of antibiotics. The mechanism of TC degradation could potentially include both radical and non-radical pathways, and degradation could be monitored in real-time. A dual-function platform based on NS-NCDs for detection and catalysis was successfully established to overcome significant barriers to visual on-site monitoring and simultaneous catalytic degradation.

In the present study, the hydro/solvothermal method was used. Thiosemicarbazide and ASA were combined in a 1:1 M ratio, which was the optimum proportion compared with ratios of 1:2 and 2:1, and the mixed solution was dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water under ultrasonication. Subsequently, the mixed solution was transferred to a hydrothermal synthesis reactor and allowed to react at 140, 170, and 200 °C for 4, 6, and 8 h, respectively. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant obtained after centrifugation was filtrated (0.22 μm aqueous filter membrane) to eliminate large particle contaminants. The collected filtrate was dialyzed for 48 h in a 1000 Da dialysis bag to remove small molecular impurities. Dialyzed product was processed through freeze-drying for dehydration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of optimal synthesis and detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To establish an efficient fluorescence sensor detection system, synthesis conditions, including reaction time and temperature, were optimized. Moreover, the detection conditions, including the probe concentration, excitation wavelength, and emission wavelength, were optimized.

1 mL of TCs solution at various concentrations was individually injected into 4 mL of the probe solution, resulting in final TCs concentrations of 0–200 μM. Fluorescence spectrum analysis was performed to establish a correlation curve and to calculate the detection limit according to the fluorescence intensity of NS-NCDs as the TC concentration increased.

To assess the selectivity of the nanoprobe for TCs detection, fluorescence experiments were simultaneously conducted with Ag+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Cl−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, sulfamethoxazole (SMZ), L-lysine (LYS), D-glutamic acid (GLU), glycine (GLY), streptomycin (SM), ampicillin sodium (AMP), Norfloxacin (NOR), Roxithromycin (ROX) and Vancomycin (VAN). Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of NS-NCDs in response to TCs was investigated in the presence of these ions or antibiotics.

The tap water and milk utilized in this study offered a realistic environment for TCs detection, but both samples required specific pre-treatment before the testing experiments. For tap water samples, the sole requirement is membrane filtration to eliminate large-particle impurities. Milk samples were obtained from a nearby supermarket. Milk was routinely pretreated as described previously (Hou et al., 2016). First, 20 mL acetonitrile was mixed with 10 mL milk under continuous stirring for 30 min. The thoroughly stirred mixture was then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min, and the filtered experiment was performed on the collected supernatant. Finally, samples with realistic pollutant characteristics were prepared by introducing various concentrations of standard TC solutions into the processed samples.

The photocatalytic degradation experiments were conducted in a 250 mL circulating water thermostatic reactor using a 300 W xenon lamp to simulate sunlight. 0.03 g/L of NS-NCDs was incorporated into 100 mL of TCs (20 mg/L) solution, and the mixture was stirred in the dark for 60 min to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Following the addition of the PMS solution, the xenon lamp was turned on immediately away from the photocatalytic degradation experiment. 1 mL samples were taken at 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 60, and 80 min. Afterward, 1 mL of sodium thiosulfate was added as a quencher, the mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter, and the TCs concentration in the filtrate was analyzed.

To further investigate the catalytic activity of NS-NCDs, the apparent rate constant of TCs degradation was computed by fitting a pseudo-first-order equation. Eq. (1)

In this study, a single-step hydrothermal method was used for NS-NCDs synthesis. Different reaction durations and temperatures were considered to enhance the optical properties of NS-NCDs. As the synthesis temperature was increased, the fluorescence intensity of the NC-NCDs progressively increased in the same reaction time batch (Fig. S1). Furthermore, among the NS-NCDs synthesized in multiple batches, the batch with the highest fluorescence intensity exhibited a reaction time of 6 h. The optimal synthesis temperature was 200 °C, with a reaction duration of 6 h.

NS-NCDs synthesized under the optimal conditions were characterized. The external dimensions of the NS-NCDs were observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The NS-NCDs were uniformly dispersed without significant agglomeration, and they exhibited an approximately spherical shape measuring 2.7 nm in average diameter (Fig. 1 a and b). HRTEM (in the inset of Fig. 1a) results for the NS-NCDs indicated a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, demonstrated good crystallinity and were consistent with the (100) plane of graphite carbon (Zhao et al., 2020). In the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the NS-NCDs from Fig. 1c, a broad peak centered at 2θ = 24.5° was observed, attributed to the graphite lattice. The shift in the NS-NCDs diffraction peak, in comparison with the typical (002) peak at 26.5°, was likely due to a decline in the ordered sp2 layers, which depended on the high-defect carbon structure on the NS-NCDs (Yang et al., 2021). The chemical composition of the NS-NCDs was further characterized by Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The broadband at 3480 cm−1 in the NS-NCDs spectrum (Fig. 1d) corresponded to the overlapping stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H groups, while the peak at 2923 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of the C–H bond (Xing et al., 2021). A major peak at 2080 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching model of the -SCN bond. The absorption bands centered around 1600, 1400, and 1200 cm−1 corresponded to the C=O/C=N, C–N, and C–O bonds, respectively (Cui, X. et al., 2022; Qi et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2023). Bands at 600 cm−1 represented the S=O stretching vibration (Yusuf et al., 2021). The chemical composition of NS-NCDs was investigated via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As depicted in Fig. 1e, the XPS survey spectrum of NS-NCDs exhibited four characteristic peaks at 150, 300, 400, and 580 eV, corresponding to S 2p, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively (Sun et al., 2018). The percentages of C, N, O, and S were calculated as 62.44 %, 18.86 %, 14.14 %, and 4.56 %, respectively. Moreover, the atomic contents of N and S were significantly higher than those reported in the literature, which is beneficial for the improvement of the fluorescence properties (Amjadi et al., 2017). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) could be split into four main peaks of C-C/C=C, C-O, C-S/C-N, and C=O/C=N, at 284.38, 284.98, 285.86, and 287.62 eV, respectively (Yan et al., 2024; Zhao et al., 2020). The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) was divided into four characteristic peaks of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, N-H, and graphitic N, at 398.80, 399.35, 400.03, and 400.85 eV, respectively. Some studies have indicated that the configurations of C-N in NS-NCDs could substantially affect the fluorescence intensity owing to the influence of delocalized π electrons from pyridinic N (Wang et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2021). Additionally, the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1h) exhibited three fitted peaks at 530.94, 531.74, and 532.61 eV, which corresponded to N-O/O-H, C-O, and C=O. The high-resolution S 2p spectrum (Fig. 1i) showed four peaks at 161.49, 163.86, 164.93, and 168.54 eV, which were attributed to the binding energies of S2O3 2−, C-S 3/2, C-S 1/2, and SO4 2−, respectively (Cui et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022b; Zhong et al., 2023). The XPS results demonstrated that NS-NCDs were successfully prepared and contained various N-, S-, and O-containing chemical groups, consistent with the FTIR results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NS-NCDs analysis of optical properties and establishment of detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy methods were utilized to study the optical properties of NS-NCDs. The intensity and peak positions of the fluorescence emission depended on the excitation wavelength (Fig. S2a). The emission peak underwent a gradual redshift toward longer wavelengths as the excitation wavelength increased (the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were located at 395 and 471 nm, respectively). In addition, using quinine sulfate as a standard reference, the relative QY of NS-NCDs at 395 nm was found to be 11.32 % (Table S1), which was higher than the quantum yields of NCDs reported in other studies (Table S2). The synthesized NS-NCDs displayed visible blue fluorescence to the naked eye under 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) irradiation (Fig. S2b). However, the NS-NCD solution exhibited no perceptible fluorescence and remained transparent and pale yellow under daylight irradiation. The fluorescence intensity of the NS-NCDs remained stable without a significant decrease after 30 days (Fig. S3a). Additionally, the fluorescence response of NS-NCDs to variations in pH, temperature, and ionic strength was depicted in Figs. S3b, c, and d. The data revealed that NS-NCDs were able to maintain a high fluorescence intensity even in different environmental conditions. These results collectively confirm the superior structural integrity and photostability of NS-NCDs under diverse environmental stressors, validating their suitability for practical applications requiring long-term operational reliability.

Furthermore, the effect of NS-NCDs concentration on fluorescence intensity was studied (Fig. S2c). With the concentration of NS-NCDs increasing from 0.01 g/L to 0.03 g/L, the fluorescence intensity gradually grew because of the higher number of NS-NCDs. The fluorescence intensity of the detection system progressively decreased with an increase in the NS-NCDs concentration from 0.03 g/L to 0.13 g/L, because blue fluorescence mainly originated from surface defects in the NS-NCDs. Consequently, the Zeta potential of NS-NCDs solutions at different concentrations was assessed (Fig. S3e). Fig. S3e revealed that as the concentration of NS-NCDs increased, the Zeta potential dropped, which implied that higher concentrations facilitated more aggregation of the NS-NCDs. This aggregation might have led to a reduction in the number of available surface defects, subsequently affecting the fluorescence intensity (Zaini et al., 2024). In summary, the optimal concentration of NS-NCDs was 0.03 mg/L, which was used for subsequent performance investigations. Furthermore, the response of NS-NCDs to TCs was also investigated (Fig. S2d). Although an earlier part of the study indicated that the optimal excitation wavelength of NS-NCDs was 395 nm, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of NS-NCDs upon exposure to TC, OTC, and CTC was low at this wavelength. Nevertheless, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the NS-NCDs increased significantly at excitation wavelengths ≤370 nm. This phenomenon can be attributed to the IFE. A more detailed analysis is presented in this study. In the absence of TC, OTC, and CTC (Fig. S2a), NS-NCDs showed no significant change in fluorescence intensity at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm compared to that at 390 nm. Therefore, 370 nm was the optimal excitation wavelength and 462 nm was the optimal emission wavelength.

To investigate the relationship between TCs concentration and fluorescence intensity of NS-NCDs, the NS-NCDs system was supplemented with varying concentrations of TC, OTC, and CTC. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the fluorescence intensity of NS-NCDs gradually declined as the TC concentration increased from 0 to 200 μM, and an outstanding curve fit relationship was observed between TC concentration (0–50 μM/50–200 μM) and fluorescence intensity at 462 nm. The fitted equations (Fig. 2d) were F/F 0 = 0.9818 − 0.0091C[TC] (R 2 = 0.995) and F/F 0 = 0.067 + 0.46/[1 + (C[TC]/93.32)3.767] (R 2 = 0.992), and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.21 μM (signal-to-noise ratio = 3). Additionally, the fluorescence response to OTC was similar to that of TC (Fig. 2b), demonstrating excellent linear and nonlinear relationships between NS-NCDs fluorescence intensity and OTC concentration within the ranges of 0–20 μM and 20–200 μM, respectively. The fitted equations (Fig. 2e) were F/F 0 = 0.99756–0.01005C[OTC] (R 2 = 0.983) and F/F 0 = −0.0029 + 0.891/[1 + (C[OTC]/72.60)1.707] (R 2 = 0.998), and the LOD was 0.28 μM. Fig. 2c illustrates the correlation between the fluorescence intensity of NS-NCDs and the concentration of added CTC. A linear relationship and a nonlinear relationship (Fig. 2f) existed between F/F 0 and the added CTC concentration in the ranges of 0–15 μM and 15–200 μM. The linear and nonlinear equations can be calculated separately as F/F 0 = 0.99741–0.0122C[CTC] (R 2 = 0.991) and F/F 0 = −0.182 + 1.117/[1 + (C[CTC]/86.78)1.233] (R 2 = 0.997), while the LOD was determined as 0.16 μM. The obtained LODs were below the maximum residue limit for TCs (0.676 μM) in the environment as stipulated by the US Food and Drug Administration. With increasing TCs concentration, the fluorescence color of the system transitioned gradually from blue to colorless, as shown in Fig. 2g, h and i. As a result, NS-NCDs exhibited potential as a fluorescence probe for the visual semi-quantification of TCs. Fig. 2g, h and i shows the standard color charts for TC, OTC and CTC for reference. Table S3 presents a comparison of various fluorescence sensors for TCs detection. NS-NCDs demonstrated good linearity and a LOD comparable to those reported in other studies. Meanwhile, the R 2 of the nonlinear curves fitted in this work was close to 1, which indicated that NS-NCDs could provide a larger TCs detection range. In addition, the response time of probe NS-NCDs toward TCs was analyzed. As shown in Fig. S4, NS-NCDs achieved a fast response toward TCs.

Selectivity and anti-interference features are important indexes for evaluating the feasibility of detection sensors in complex samples. Hence, the fluorescence responses of NS-NCDs toward TCs, Ag+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Cl−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, MZ, LYS, GLU, GLY, SM, AMP, NOR, ROX, and VAN were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence of NS-NCDs in the system was significantly quenched when only TCs were present, and no significant signal change in 462 nm was observed when the NS-NCD solution was incubated with interfering substances. Moreover, anti-interference experiments were conducted in the presence of both the mentioned interfering substances and TCs. The experimental results suggested that the impacts of external substances on the fluorescence response of the probe to TC (Fig. 3b), OTC (Fig. 3c), and CTC (Fig. 3d) were negligible. In summary, NS-NCDs exhibited remarkable selectivity and anti-interference properties for TCs, which provided a strong foundation for establishing a promising novel TCs detection method.

According to previous studies, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the NS-NCDs sensor typically involves fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), IFE, and photo-induced electron transfer (Lang et al., 2018; Qiu et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2019). To elucidate the fluorescence quenching mechanism in NS-NCDs, the relationship between the UV–vis spectrum of TCs and the fluorescence spectrum of the NS-NCDs was further explored. The UV–vis results (Fig. 4 a) indicated that the overlapping portion between the absorption spectrum of the TC solution and the emission spectrum of NS-NCDs could be neglected, thus excluding the FRET mechanism. Nevertheless, the effective adsorption of excitation light by TCs at 370 nm led to a reduction in excitation light intensity received by NS-NCDs, consequently resulting in fluorescence quenching. This resulted in a significant quenching of the fluorescence of the NS-NCDs sensor. The results indicated that IFE was a possible mechanism for the quenching of NS-NCDs by TCs.

To further explore the proportion of IFE quenching efficiency in the total quenching efficiency, IFE was estimated using Eq. (2) (Liu et al., 2016):

obsd ) for different concentrations (0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 μM) of TC, OTC, and CTC were calculated (Table 1 ). Subsequently, the computed data were utilized to draw Fig. 4b, which illustrated the observed inhibition efficiency (Eobsd) of TCs and the suppression efficiency obtained after the removal of IFE correction (Ecorr). IFE inhibition efficiency (EIFE) was determined by the difference between the Eobsd and the corrected Ecorr (EIFE = Eobsd - Ecorr). Hence, the ratio of EIFE/Eobsd was 76 % for TC, 75 % for OTC, and 71 % for CTC, indicating the involvement of other quenching mechanisms alongside IFE. In addition, the changes in the UV–vis spectrum of the system after the addition of TC, OTC, and CTC (Fig. 5 a, b, and c, respectively) revealed that NS-NCDs reacted with TCs to form complexes. Moreover, the interaction force strongly induced the transfer of excited state electrons from -NH2/-COOH groups on the surface of NS-NCDs to -CO- and -OH groups of TCs, resulting in fluorescence quenching (Zhang and Chen, 2016). To further investigate the main causes of fluorescence quenching, the lifetimes of NS-NCDs before and after quenching were measured, using the NS-NCDs + OTC system as an example. The measurement results are presented in Table S4 and Fig. 5d. The fluorescence lifetimes of NS-NCDs and the mixed solution after OTC addition were 5.12 and 3.89 ns, respectively, indicating significant electron transfer between NS-NCDs and TCs (Liu et al., 2022). Therefore, the key mechanisms underlying the dynamic quenching of NS-NCDs were IFE and electron transfer.

To evaluate the applicability of the NS-NCDs sensor in practical samples, detection and quantification of TCs in both tap water and milk samples were conducted. Milk and tap water samples were analyzed by injecting various concentrations of TCs (5, 10, and 20 μM). Recovery rates of TCs in milk and tap water samples were 89.4 %–111.4 % and 96.2 %–116.2 %, respectively, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs, n = 3) were low (Table S5). These results demonstrated the high accuracy and excellent reliability of the proposed NS-NCDs sensor in detecting TCs in food and the environment.

Light-assisted PMS activation experiments were carried out to evaluate the degradation efficiencies (Fig. 6 a) and apparent kinetic constants (Fig. 6b) of NS-NCDs on TC, OTC, and CTC under various conditions. Fig. 6a illustrated that in the first 60 min, the adsorption efficiency of NS-NCDs for TCs under dark conditions was found to be just 4 %., with the subsequent removal of TCs being almost entirely attributed to photocatalytic degradation. Taking TC as an example, Regarding the degradation test, the removal efficiency of TC by NS-NCDs was nearly 0 over 80 min when only light was present. In the absence of light, PMS activated by NS-NCDs could rapidly degrade over 85 % of TC within 60 min, with an apparent kinetic constant of 0.032 min−1 (Details are in Table S6). Moreover, upon introducing light to the NS-NCDs/PMS system, both the TC removal rate and degradation rate constant increased with the amount of PMS. At a PMS dosage of 0.2 g/L, the TC removal rate achieved 95.29 %, which was approximately 10 % higher than in the dark, with an apparent kinetic constant of 0.050 min−1. Furthermore, in the NS-NCDs/PMS/light system, the removal efficiencies for OTC and CTC reached 99.71 % and 90 %, respectively, with apparent kinetic constants of 0.077 min−1 and 0.033 min−1. These values surpass those of previously reported catalysts under comparable conditions (Table S7). Additionally, fluorescence tests were carried out on the three solutions (NS-NCDs, NS-NCDs + TC, NS-NCDs + TC + PMS) (Fig. S5a). It was observed that the fluorescence of the NS-NCDs + TC + PMS solution had recovered after 80 min of light exposure, and the inset in Fig. S5a offered a more direct visualization of the fluorescence changes, demonstrating that the degradation process was observable in real-time with the naked eye during the practical operation. The experimental results indicated that NS-NCDs could serve both as a fluorescent probe for the detection of TCs and as a catalyst for their degradation, effectively integrating in situ detection and catalytic degradation.

To further investigate the interference resistance of NS-NCDs as a catalyst in external environments, TC was used as an example to test the removal efficiencies (Fig. 6c) and apparent kinetic constants (Fig. 6d) of TC under different pH conditions (Details are in Table S8). The experimental data revealed that the degradation efficiency of TC increased gradually as the pH of the solution was raised from 3 to 9. This effect was attributed to the suppression of free radical formation (SO4 •- and •OH) by excessive H+ in acidic environments (Huang et al., 2021). In addition, Fig. S3f revealed that the pH point of zero charge (pHpzc) for the NS-NCDs was about 3.1, implying that the surface of the NS-NCDs carried a positive charge in highly acidic conditions. Previous studies indicated that protonation of amino groups in TC under strong acid condition generated positive charges, which created electrostatic repulsion with NCDs and impeded electron transfer, thereby reducing degradation efficiency (Cao et al., 2018). As the pH of the solution was increased to 11, the degradation efficiency of TC was rapidly enhanced, reaching 99 % in 10 min, with the apparent kinetic constant increasing to 0.506 min−1. It was indicated by research that, in a strongly alkaline environment, the hydroxyl and amino groups in the TCs molecule might have deprotonated and undergone hydrolysis in the alkaline environment, which led to the destruction of the molecular structure and an acceleration of the degradation process (Li et al., 2020b). In addition, the apparent molar adsorption coefficient (εPMS) showed little change under acidic, neutral, and weakly alkaline conditions. Nonetheless, εPMS markedly increased at pH ≥ 9, which could boost the decomposition of PMS (Ao et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2008). Overall, the NS-NCDs/PMS/light system maintains high TC removal efficiency across a broad pH range, supporting its feasibility for diverse aquatic environments.

To facilitate a better understanding of the possible mechanisms for TC degradation, the band structure of the NS-NCDs was examined. Initially, the bandgap energy (Eg) was calculated as 2.97 eV from the UV absorption spectrum and Tauc plots of the NS-NCDs (Fig. S6a). Subsequently, the Mott-Schottky (Fig. S6b) analysis revealed a flat-band potential of −1.16 V for the NS-NCDs, and the positive slope of the curve verified their n-type semiconductor characteristics. Consequently, the conduction band (CB) potential was approximately 0.1 V lower than the flat-band potential, giving a CB value of −1.26 V (Choi et al., 2022). Through the mathematical relationship of the semiconductor bandgap energy values, valence band (VB) energy (EVB), and conduction band energy (ECB): EVB = Eg + ECB, it was calculated that the EVB of NS-NCDs was 1.71 eV (Li et al., 2022).

Studies from the past commonly established that SO4 •-, •OH, and O2 •- were major active species in AOPs (Li et al., 2017a). However, recent articles pointed out that carbon-based materials could directly oxidize organic pollutants through electron transfer (Duan et al., 2014; Lee et al., 2015). Therefore, this study aimed to elucidate the contributions of free radicals and non-free radicals within the NS-NCDs/PMS/light system by evaluating the effect of different scavengers on PMS activation. Generally, MeOH was recognized as a scavenger for both SO4 •- and •OH; tert-butanol (TBA) was a scavenger of •OH; benzoquinone (BQ) was a scavenger of O2 •-; ammonium oxalate (AO) was a scavenger of h+; AgNO3 scavenger of e−. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the degradation efficiency of TC was inhibited by 18 %, 7 %, and 14 % when 50 mM MeOH, TBA, and BQ were added to the system, respectively. This suggested that these three radicals were all involved to some degree in the oxidation of TC. To further confirm the involvement of the three types of free radicals in the oxidation of TC, EPR experiments were conducted using DMPO as a spin-trapping agent, as shown in Fig. 7c and d). Characteristic signals associated with DMPO-SO4 •-, DMPO-•OH, and DMPO-O2 •- were detected, but with relatively low intensity. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. S5b, the characteristic signal of TEMP-1O2 was absent in the NS-NCDs/PMS/light system, suggesting that 1O2 played a negligible role in the degradation process of TC. Overall, while SO4 •-, •OH, and O2 •- participate in the degradation of TC, they may not be the primary mechanism.

Meanwhile, the addition of AO (50 mM) not only improved the TC removal rate but also greatly accelerated the degradation process (k = 0.162 min−1, Fig. 7b, details are in Table S9). Subsequent experiments using TEMPO as a spin trap to capture h+ revealed a strong TEMPO-h+ characteristic peak in Fig. 7e, demonstrating that a substantial amount of h+ was produced by the NS-NCDs/PMS/light system. Test results clearly showed that the oxidative capacity of h+ towards TC was relatively poor. The increased degradation efficiency was mainly attributed to AO consuming h+ on the surface of the catalyst, which reduced the likelihood of e−-h+ recombination (Han et al., 2020). This, in turn, led to more e− rapidly migrating to the surface of the reactants through the unique zero-dimensional structure of the NS-NCDs, thereby enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. In contrast, when 50 mM of AgNO3 was added to the NS-NCDs/PMS/light system, the TC removal rate was merely 15 % within 80 min, indicating that AgNO3 effectively trapped photogenerated e− and greatly impeded the degradation efficiency of TC. Hence, the results indicated that the degradation of TC was mainly due to the e− transfer between the catalyst, PMS, and TC.

To elucidate the primary mechanism of NS-NCDs in activating PMS for TC degradation, the NS-NCDs after multiple photocatalytic cycles were systematically characterized using FTIR, XPS, and XRD. As shown in Fig. S7a, the FTIR spectra revealed a significant enhancement of the C-O peak at 1180 cm−1 after the reaction, consistent with the increased C-O-related peak areas in both C 1s (Fig. S7b) and O 1s (Fig. S7d) XPS spectra (Gao et al., 2022). These results collectively confirmed the participation of surface defects and edge carbon sites in PMS activation. Concurrently, the weakened amino/hydroxyl peaks at 3400 cm−1 in FTIR, coupled with substantial reductions in O-H (O 1s) and N-H (N 1s) XPS peak areas (Fig. S7c), strongly demonstrated that surface amino and hydroxyl groups served as critical active pathways for PMS activation (Chen et al., 2021). Furthermore, the decrease in pyridinic N and the increase in graphitic N observed in the N1s XPS spectra suggested that pyridinic N might facilitate the cleavage of the O-O bond in PMS by providing lone electron pairs (Lee et al., 2016; Li et al., 2020b). Notably, the emergence of S=O bonds (600 cm−1) in FTIR and corresponding S 2p XPS (Fig. S7e) signals indicated the adsorption of sulfate ions on the NS-NCDs surface after repeated cycles, aligning with the interfacial accumulation of sulfur species. These findings systematically clarify the synergistic roles of surface functional groups, nitrogen configurations, and defect sites in the PMS activation mechanism. Based on the above analysis, three possible mechanisms for the activation of PMS by NS-NCDs in the oxidation of TC were proposed (see Scheme 1 ). First, PMS can generate highly active free radicals (SO4 •-, •OH, and O2 •-) under light irradiation through self-decomposition and activation by photogenerated e− (Eqs. (3) and (4)) (Li et al., 2022). Second, the surface-active sites of carbon-based materials catalyze the decomposition of PMS, producing reactive free radicals (Eqs. (5)–(11)) (Ghanbari and Moradi, 2017). Third, NS-NCDs act as an electron-transfer mediator between TC and activated PMS, facilitating indirect TC degradation via a non-radical pathway without free radical generation.

In addition, the cycling stability of NS-NCDs in photocatalytic applications was evaluated (Fig. 7f). Data showed that after five cycles, the degradation efficiency of TC remained around 80 % within 60 min, confirming the good stability of NS-NCDs. As shown in Fig. S7f, the XRD patterns revealed a discernible shift in the (002) peak position and a reduction in peak intensity after the reaction. Notably, no emergence of new peaks or disappearance of existing peaks was observed, indicating that the NS-NCDs retained their primary structure without significant phase transformation or impurity formation during multiple catalytic cycles, thereby maintaining structural stability. Considering the complexity and diversity of real-world environments, photocatalytic experiments based on real water samples were conducted. Fig. S8 revealed that 80 min of reaction resulted in 89.34 %, 83.74 %, and 92.77 % decomposition efficiencies for TC, CTC, and OTC, respectively. Compared to Fig. 6a, although the final degradation rates of the three TCs showed a slight decrease under the same amount of addition, they still maintained a high removal efficiency. This further validated the feasibility and stability of the NS-NCDs as photocatalysts in real-world environments.

The intermediates formed during TC degradation were characterized by LC-MS (Fig. S9). Based on the mass spectrometric analysis results, the degradation pathway of TC in the NS-NCDs/PMS/light photocatalytic system was revealed, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 8 . In Pathway I, P1 first underwent dehydration to form P2, which was then converted to ring-opened P3 through active species attack, followed by decarboxylation and functional group elimination reactions to yield P4 (Gao et al., 2023). Pathway Ⅱ revealed that TC was attacked by O2 •-, leading to the loss of the methyl group and decomposition into P5. P5 then underwent a deamination reaction, resulting in the formation of P9 (Pathway Ⅲ) (Hu et al., 2024). In addition, after the amino, methyl, and hydroxyl groups were separated, P5 was converted into P6, which then underwent a ring-opening reaction to form P7 (Fang et al., 2022). The final step involved the opening of the third phenyl ring, producing a low-molecular-weight P8 (Pathway Ⅳ). The relatively low-molecular-weight intermediates were ultimately converted into CO2, H2O and NH4 +.

Dual-functional NS-NCDs with blue fluorescence were synthesized through the incorporation of thiosemicarbazide as a dopant and ASA as a nitrogen source, and the structure and surface functional groups of the NS-NCDS were characterized. The synthesized NS-NCDs exhibited high water solubility, high fluorescence intensity, and high photostability. Furthermore, the NS-NCDs acted as fluorescent probes with high selectivity, anti-interference, and sensitivity for the efficient detection of TC, OTC, and CTC. Specifically, the LODs were 0.21 μM (TC), 0.28 μM (OTC), and 0.16 μM (CTC). These values are comparable to the LODs reported in other studies on TC detection. IFE and electron transfer were the key mechanisms underlying the dynamic quenching of NS-NCDs fluorescence by TCs. In addition, the combination of NS-NCDs as a catalyst with PMS under sunlight resulted in over 90 % degradation of the three TCs. The mechanism behind NS-NCDs as catalysts included, first, the activation of PMS to create SO4 •-, •OH, and O2 •-; and second, the direct extraction of electrons from TCs by PMS adsorbed on surface defects of the NS-NCDs, which promoted the degradation of pollutants. Overall, this work provides a new idea for NS-NCDs preparation and creatively combines TCs with photocatalytic degradation, offering significant practical value.

Baocun Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Shuguang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.
